risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "practitioners will be stratified according to whether they are urban or rural and block randomisation with random block sizes will be used within each stratum. The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study" Comment: This describes a proper random allocation procedure (stratified block randomisation with random block sizes) and explicitly states the randomisation was carried out by a statistician, which is sufficient for Low risk.

Quote: "The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners. Allocation of clusters to intervention or comparison will done following collection of baseline data. In other words, at the time of screening and recruitment of women, the allocation of GPs (and therefore, of women) will be unknown." Comment: This explicitly states that allocation was handled by an independent statistician and, crucially, that allocation was unknown during screening and recruitment, which indicates concealment from recruiters and prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention (professional training plus patient counselling) it is not possible to blind the GPs to their status as intervention or control. Similarly... women are not blinded..." Comment: Participants and key personnel were explicitly not blinded, and the outcomes assessed (e.g. quality of life, mental health, safety behaviours, comfort to discuss abuse, GP inquiry) could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation and differential behaviour/care. This therefore represents high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "There is no blinding as regards data collection based on the CONSORT guidelines [50], as the women and GPs themselves complete the surveys (i.e. data were not collected by a research assistant blinded to the allocation)." Comment: The outcome assessors were the participants themselves (women self-reporting via postal questionnaires) and the GPs for doctor-related outcomes; the paper explicitly states data collection was not blinded. Because the main outcomes for women include subjective/self-reported measures (quality of life, mental health, safety behaviours, comfort discussing abuse), knowledge of allocation could influence responses and therefore detection bias is possible. For the doctor outcomes, the GPs were also not blinded and the outcomes are judgement-/questionnaire-based, so bias is likewise plausible.

Quote: "Following randomisation, approximately a third (88) will be lost to follow-up at 12 months based on data from the diamond cohort [51] and MOSAIC [52] leaving 89 women per group at 12 months." Comment: The study anticipated substantial attrition of about one-third by 12 months, which is above the usual threshold for low risk. In addition, the review characteristics report withdrawals/dropouts among women (intervention n = 41, control n = 35), suggesting notable missing outcome data in both arms. Although the analysis was planned as intention-to-treat and mixed/GEE models were mentioned, the amount of missing follow-up data is large enough that attrition could plausibly bias the results.
